topics.name: JavaScript Fundamentals

var, let, const and scope: var: function-scoped, hoisted to top of function, can be re-declared. let: block-scoped, not hoisted usably, cannot be re-declared in same scope. const: block-scoped, must be initialised immediately, cannot be reassigned.
Block scope means the variable only exists inside the nearest { }. This prevents accidental overwrites — the #1 reason to prefer let/const over var in React code.
Data types and type coercion: Primitive types: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint. Reference types: object, array, function.
Type coercion: JS silently converts types when operators are used. '5' + 3 = '53' (string concat). '5' - 3 = 2 (numeric). Always use === (strict equality) in React to avoid coercion bugs.
Truthy and Falsy values: Falsy values (6 only): false, 0, '' (empty string), null, undefined, NaN. Everything else is truthy — including '0', [], {}.
Critical for React: conditional rendering uses truthy/falsy. {count && <Badge />} renders nothing when count = 0 (falsy) but renders when count = 1+. Use {count > 0 && <Badge />} to be explicit.
Operators and expressions: Ternary operator: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse — most used in React JSX for conditional rendering.

Optional chaining (?.) : user?.address?.city — safely accesses nested properties without crashing if any level is null/undefined. Nullish coalescing (??) : value ?? 'default' — uses default only if value is null or undefined (not for 0 or '').
Control flow: if-else, switch, loops: if/else works normally in JS logic but CANNOT be used directly inside JSX return. Use ternary or logical && inside JSX instead.
Array methods like .map(), .filter(), .reduce() are preferred over for loops in React because they return new arrays (immutable pattern) and work directly inside JSX.

common_mistakes: Using var inside React components — always use const by default, let when reassignment needed, never var
Using == instead of === — always use strict equality in React to avoid type coercion bugs

topics.name: Functions and Arrow Functions

Function declarations vs expressions: Function declaration: function greet() {} — hoisted, available before definition in code. Function expression: const greet = function() {} — not hoisted, only available after definition.
In React, components are almost always written as function expressions (const MyComponent = () => {}) or arrow functions for consistency.
Arrow functions and this context: Arrow functions do NOT have their own 'this' — they inherit 'this' from the surrounding scope. Regular functions have their own 'this' which changes based on how they are called.

In React class components, event handlers lose 'this' context. Fix: use arrow functions as class properties OR bind in constructor. In functional components (hooks), 'this' is never needed — arrow functions preferred throughout.
Default parameters: Default parameter: function greet(name = 'Guest') {} — if name is not passed or is undefined, 'Guest' is used. Used heavily in React component prop defaults before defaultProps.
Callback functions: Callback: a function passed as argument to another function, called later. Foundation of React event handling: onClick={handleClick}. Also core to array methods: .map(item => <Li key={item.id} />).

common_mistakes: Using regular functions as React event handlers in class components — they lose 'this'. Use arrow function class properties instead
Arrow function returning an object: () => {id: 1} is WRONG (treats {} as function body). Write () => ({id: 1}) with parentheses

topics.name: Arrays and Objects (ES6+)

map, filter, reduce: map: transforms every element, returns NEW array of same length. filter: keeps elements matching condition, returns NEW array. reduce: combines all elements into single value.

These are the core tools for rendering lists in React (.map()), filtering data (.filter()), and computing derived state (.reduce()). All return new arrays — never mutate original (immutability rule in React).
Object shorthand: If property name equals variable name: {name: name, age: age} can be written as {name, age}. Used everywhere in React: setState({loading, data, error}) is much cleaner.
Computed property names: {[dynamicKey]: value} — useful for updating specific fields in state dynamically.
Destructuring: Array destructuring: const [first, second] = array. The [state, setState] = useState() pattern IS array destructuring.
Object destructuring: const {name, age} = user. In React: const {title, onClick, children} = props — extract exactly what you need. Rename: const {name: userName} = user.
Spread operator: Spread array: [...arr1, ...arr2] creates new merged array. Spread object: {...obj1, ...obj2} creates new merged object. Later properties override earlier ones.
Immutable state updates in React: setUser({...user, name: 'Bob'}) — creates new object with all old fields plus updated name. NEVER mutate state directly: user.name = 'Bob' breaks React.

common_mistakes: Mutating state directly: state.items.push(newItem) — React won't detect this change. Use setItems([...items, newItem]) instead
Using index as key in .map() — causes bugs with reordering. Always use a unique id: products.map(p => <div key={p.id}>)

topics.name: Introduction to React
subtopics: Understanding the config file of webpack and babel

What is ReactJS & its features?: React is a JavaScript library (not framework) built by Facebook/Meta in 2013 for building user interfaces. It handles ONLY the View layer — not routing or state management (those need separate libraries).

Key features: Component-based (UI split into reusable pieces), Virtual DOM (fast updates), Declarative (describe WHAT to render, React figures out HOW), Unidirectional data flow (data flows one way: parent to child via props).
Why react came into picture?: Before React: directly manipulating the real DOM was slow and complex. jQuery helped but large apps became spaghetti code — no structure, no reuse.
React solved: performance (Virtual DOM diffs only what changed), structure (component tree), reusability (same component used anywhere), developer experience (JSX makes UI code readable).
Single Page Application (SPA): Traditional (MPA): every click loads a new HTML page from server — slow, full page refresh. SPA: one HTML file loaded once, JS dynamically updates only the changed part — fast, no reload.
React SPAs: React Router handles navigation by swapping components, not pages. URL changes but browser never makes a new request to server for the page.
Understanding Webpack and Babel: Babel: transpiler that converts modern JS (ES6+, JSX) to older JS that all browsers understand. Without Babel, browsers can't read JSX (<div>) or arrow functions in older environments.
Webpack: module bundler. Takes hundreds of JS files, CSS, images → bundles into few optimised files (bundle.js). Also handles hot reload during development.
Understanding the config file of webpack: babel.config.json: lists presets (@babel/preset-env for modern JS, @babel/preset-react for JSX). Create React App (CRA) and Vite configure this automatically — you rarely touch it directly.

webpack.config.js: entry point, output path, loaders (rules for which files Babel processes), plugins (HtmlWebpackPlugin, MiniCssExtractPlugin). CRA hides this — use 'npm run eject' to expose it (irreversible).

common_mistakes: Thinking React is a full framework like Angular — React is only the View layer. You need React Router for routing and Redux/Zustand for state management separately

topics.name: Client and Server Side Applications
subtopics: Technologies used in client side application & example
Technologies used in server side application & example

Introduction to client side application: Client-side rendering (CSR): browser downloads minimal HTML, then JavaScript runs in browser to build the full UI. The server only sends data (via API), not rendered HTML.
Technologies used in client side applica: Technologies: React, Vue, Angular, Svelte. Example: Gmail — loads once, then fetches emails via API and updates UI without page reload. Fast after initial load.
Introduction to server side application: Server-side rendering (SSR): server builds complete HTML for each request and sends to browser. Browser displays HTML immediately, then JS takes over (hydration).
Technologies used in server side applica: Technologies: Next.js (React-based SSR), Nuxt (Vue), traditional PHP/Laravel, Ruby on Rails. Example: news websites — each article page generated on server for SEO.
Client side vs server side: CSR: faster after load, better interactivity, poor SEO (search bots see empty HTML), slower first load. SSR: great SEO (full HTML for bots), faster first paint, more server load.
Hybrid: Next.js gives best of both — static pages (SSG) for speed, SSR for SEO, CSR for dynamic parts.
Using react in client side: React is primarily CSR. index.html has one <div id='root'> — React's createRoot() mounts the entire app there. All routing, pages, components live in JS — never leaves the single HTML file.

required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Client-Side (CSR) | Server-Side (SSR) || First load speed | Slower (downloads all JS) | Faster (gets ready HTML) || SEO | Poor (empty HTML initially) | Excellent (full HTML) || Interactivity | Excellent after load | Good (needs hydration) || Server load | Low (only data APIs) | High (renders each page) || Example | Gmail, Figma | News sites, E-commerce || React support | Native (CRA, Vite) | Next.js
common_mistakes: React alone is not good for SEO — if SEO matters, use Next.js which adds SSR/SSG on top of React

topics.name: Setting up React Project
subtopics: Setting up development environment for react
Knowing more about node_modules, package.json, package.lock.json

Setting up development environment for r: Prerequisites: Node.js (LTS version) installed from nodejs.org. Node includes npm. Verify: node -v and npm -v in terminal.
Two modern options: Create React App (CRA) — beginner-friendly, zero config. Vite — faster, modern, recommended for new projects in 2024+.
Starter project with CDN: CDN approach: no installation needed. Add React and ReactDOM via <script> tags in HTML. Good for learning but NOT for production — no bundling, no JSX support without Babel CDN too.
Used for quick experiments or adding React to existing HTML pages. For real projects, always use CRA or Vite.
Understanding npm and yarn: npm (Node Package Manager): comes with Node. yarn: Facebook's alternative, faster (parallel installs). Both manage packages from npmjs.com registry.
Key commands: npm install / yarn add, npm run start / yarn start, npm run build / yarn build. package.json is the source of truth for which packages are used.
Create React App: CRA: npx create-react-app my-app. Vite (modern, preferred): npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react.
CRA vs Vite: CRA uses webpack (slower), Vite uses ESBuild (10-100x faster). For 2024+ projects, Vite is the community recommendation. CRA is no longer actively maintained.
Understanding the File Structure: src/: all your source code — components, pages, styles, hooks. public/: static files served as-is (favicon, index.html). node_modules/: all installed packages. src/index.js (or main.jsx): entry point — mounts React app.
Knowing more about node_modules, package: node_modules/: contains source code of every installed package. NEVER commit to git (.gitignore it). Regenerated via npm install from package.json.

package.json: your project config — name, version, scripts (start, build, test), dependencies (production) and devDependencies (development only). package-lock.json (or yarn.lock): exact version tree locked for reproducible installs across machines. ALWAYS commit this.

topics.required_code:
# ===== Setup Commands =====
# Install Node.js first from nodejs.org

# Option 1: Create React App (traditional)
npx create-react-app my-react-app
cd my-react-app
npm start       # starts dev server at localhost:3000

# Option 2: Vite (recommended for 2024+)
npm create vite@latest my-react-app -- --template react
cd my-react-app
npm install
npm run dev     # starts dev server at localhost:5173

# ===== File Structure (Vite React App) =====
# my-react-app/
# ├── node_modules/        (never commit — auto-generated)
# ├── public/              (static files)
# │   └── vite.svg
# ├── src/                 (your code lives here)
# │   ├── assets/
# │   ├── App.jsx          (root component)
# │   ├── App.css
# │   ├── main.jsx         (entry point — mounts React)
# │   └── index.css
# ├── index.html           (single HTML file)
# ├── package.json         (project config — commit this)
# ├── package-lock.json    (exact versions — commit this)
# └── vite.config.js       (Vite config)

# ===== package.json example =====
# {
#   "name": "my-react-app",
#   "scripts": {
#     "dev": "vite",
#     "build": "vite build",
#     "preview": "vite preview"
#   },
#   "dependencies": {
#     "react": "^18.2.0",
#     "react-dom": "^18.2.0"
#   },
#   "devDependencies": {
#     "vite": "^5.0.0",
#     "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.0.0"
#   }
# }
common_mistakes: Committing node_modules to git — it can be 300MB+. Always add it to .gitignore
Deleting package-lock.json — this breaks reproducible installs. Never delete it

topics.name: How React Works?
subtopics: Walkthrough of diffing and reconciliation algorithm

Virtual DOM and Real DOM: Real DOM: the actual HTML tree in browser. Direct DOM manipulation is slow — changing one element can force the browser to repaint/reflow the entire page.
Virtual DOM: a lightweight JavaScript object copy of the real DOM, kept in memory. React makes all changes to the Virtual DOM first — which is just a JS operation (very fast).
Then React compares old Virtual DOM vs new Virtual DOM (diffing), finds what actually changed, and updates ONLY those specific parts of the Real DOM. This is called reconciliation.
Rendering (createRoot() and render()): React 18 API: ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')) creates a root. Then root.render(<App />) mounts the component tree into the DOM.
This replaces React 17's ReactDOM.render() which is now deprecated. createRoot() enables React 18's concurrent features (transitions, Suspense improvements).
Walkthrough of diffing and reconciliatio: React's diffing rules: (1) Elements of different types → destroy old tree, build new one from scratch. (2) Same type → update only changed attributes. (3) Lists → use key prop to identify which items changed/moved/added.
Why key matters: without keys, React assumes list order never changes — adding item at top re-renders everything. With keys, React knows exactly which items are new/moved.

required_diagrams: Virtual DOM reconciliation diagram: clean diagram on white background. Three columns: 1) Old Virtual DOM tree (rectangles showing App > Header, Main, Footer). 2) New Virtual DOM (same tree but Main rectangle highlighted as changed). 3) Real DOM (only Main section has update arrow pointing to it). Arrow from column 1 to 2 labeled 'Diffing'. Arrow from column 2 to 3 labeled 'Reconciliation - only changed parts updated'. Plain black, white background.
common_mistakes: Using array index as key — causes bugs when list items are added/removed/reordered. Use stable unique IDs (from database or crypto.randomUUID())

topics.name: Understanding React Components and JSX
subtopics: How to define a component without JSX?
How to define a component with JSX?

Introduction to component: Component: a reusable, independent piece of UI. Like a LEGO brick — build small pieces, combine them into the full app. Every React app is a tree of components.
Rules: component name MUST start with capital letter (MyComponent not myComponent — lowercase is treated as HTML tag). Must return JSX (or null). Each component is its own file by convention.
How to define a component without JSX?: React.createElement(type, props, ...children): the underlying function JSX compiles to. Rarely written manually but important to understand — it shows JSX is just syntactic sugar.
Without JSX, nested component trees become deeply nested createElement calls — unreadable. This is exactly why JSX was invented.
What is JSX?: JSX (JavaScript XML): syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like code inside JavaScript. NOT valid JS — Babel compiles it to React.createElement calls.

JSX rules: className instead of class (class is a reserved word in JS). htmlFor instead of for. All tags must be closed (
not
). Only ONE root element per return (use Fragment <> to wrap without adding DOM node).
How to define a component with JSX?: Functional component: a JavaScript function that returns JSX. Component name in PascalCase. Props received as first argument. Must have one root element.
Embedding JS in JSX: use curly braces {}. {2 + 2} renders 4. {user.name} renders the name. {condition ? 'Yes' : 'No'} for conditionals. Cannot use if/else or for loops directly inside JSX return.

common_mistakes: Using class instead of className in JSX — Babel will show a warning, and styling won't apply
Returning multiple root elements without Fragment: return <div/><p/> is a syntax error. Wrap in <> </> or <React.Fragment>

topics.name: Types of React Components

Class Based Components: Class components: the original way to write stateful React components (before hooks in 2018). Extends React.Component. Has render() method that returns JSX. Has lifecycle methods.
Still found in large existing codebases — important to understand for maintenance. New code should use functional components + hooks.
Functional Components: Functional components: just a JavaScript function. No class, no render() method, just return JSX. With hooks (useState, useEffect), they can do everything class components do — and more cleanly.
Modern React (2019+) is entirely functional components + hooks. Simpler, less boilerplate, easier to test, better performance (no instance overhead).

required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Class Component | Functional Component || Syntax | class X extends Component | function X() or const X = () => || State | this.state + setState() | useState() hook || Lifecycle | componentDidMount etc. | useEffect() hook || this keyword | Required everywhere | Never needed || Boilerplate | More (constructor, bind) | Minimal || Performance | Slight overhead | Faster (no instance) || Modern usage | Legacy codebases | All new React code
common_mistakes: Writing new React apps with class components — all new code should use functional components. Class components are only for maintaining legacy code

topics.name: Class Based Component Deep Dive
subtopics: Introduction to class based component with example
Using state and setState in class component

Introduction to class based component wi: Class component structure: extend React.Component, implement render() method which must return JSX. The class instance ('this') holds state and methods.
class MyComponent extends React.Component — gives access to React.Component's built-in methods: setState(), forceUpdate(), and lifecycle methods.
Component constructor: constructor(props): called when component is created. Must call super(props) first — this initialises React.Component's internals and makes this.props available.
Initialise state here: this.state = { ... }. Bind event handlers here: this.handleClick = this.handleClick.bind(this). If you don't bind, 'this' inside handleClick will be undefined.
Props and props in constructor: Props passed to constructor: super(props) makes props available as this.props throughout the class. If you skip super(props) and use this.props in constructor, it will be undefined (even though it works outside constructor).
Access props: this.props.name, this.props.onClick, etc. Props are read-only — never modify this.props.
Using state and setState in class compon: this.setState(newState): merges newState into current state (shallow merge — only updates specified keys). Triggers re-render. Never mutate this.state directly.
Async setState: multiple setState calls may be batched. For state that depends on previous state, use functional form: this.setState(prevState => ({ count: prevState.count + 1 })).

common_mistakes: this.state.count++ (direct mutation) — React won't detect this change. Always use setState()
Not using functional setState when new state depends on old: this.setState({count: this.state.count + 1}) can be wrong in async batching. Use this.setState(prev => ({count: prev.count + 1}))

topics.name: Props in React

Introduction to props: Props (properties): how parent components send data to child components. Like function arguments — the parent passes, the child receives and uses.
Props are READ-ONLY — a component must never modify its own props. This enforces unidirectional data flow: data goes down (parent to child), events go up (child calls parent callback).
props vs state: Props: data coming FROM parent — external, read-only. State: data managed WITHIN the component — internal, can change via setState/setter.
Rule of thumb: if the data changes in the component itself → state. If the data comes from outside → props.
Passing data via props: Any JavaScript value can be passed as props: string, number, boolean, object, array, function. JSX: <Card title='Hello' count={42} user={userObj} tags={['a','b']} />.
Boolean shorthand: <Button disabled /> is same as <Button disabled={true} />.
Passing function via props: Parent passes a function as prop to child. Child calls it when something happens (event). This is how child communicates back to parent (lifting state up).
Pattern: parent defines handleDelete, passes as onDelete prop to child. Child calls props.onDelete(id) on button click. Parent's state updates, all children re-render with new data.
Default prop: Two ways: (1) defaultProps: Button.defaultProps = { variant: 'primary' }. (2) Default params in destructuring (preferred modern way): function Button({ variant = 'primary', size = 'md' }).
Props validation: PropTypes: runtime type checking in development. Shows warnings in console if wrong type passed. Install: npm install prop-types. Import: import PropTypes from 'prop-types'.
Inbuilt validator: PropTypes.string, PropTypes.number, PropTypes.bool, PropTypes.func, PropTypes.array, PropTypes.object, PropTypes.node (anything renderable), PropTypes.element (React element), PropTypes.oneOf(['sm','md','lg']), PropTypes.arrayOf(PropTypes.number), PropTypes.shape({name: PropTypes.string}).
Add .isRequired to make prop mandatory: PropTypes.string.isRequired
Custom validator: Custom validator: a function that receives (props, propName, componentName) and returns an Error if invalid, or null if valid.
Example: validate email format, validate that a number is positive, validate that array has at least one item.

common_mistakes: Mutating props inside a child component — props are read-only. If you need to change data, call a callback function passed from parent
Using PropTypes in production bundle — PropTypes are for development only. TypeScript is preferred for production-grade type safety

topics.name: Nesting Components

Component composition pattern: Composition: building complex UIs by combining simpler components. A Page uses Layout which uses Navbar, Sidebar, Main — each a separate component with one responsibility.
React favors composition over inheritance. Instead of extending a base component, wrap components and pass behaviour via props or children.
The children prop:
children: special React prop — whatever you put BETWEEN component's opening and closing tags becomes children.

Hello

 — the

is Card's children prop.
Makes wrapper/layout components: Card, Modal, Button with icon — any component that wraps other content. Access via props.children or destructured { children }.
Building component trees: Think top-down: App → Page → Section → Card → Button. Data flows down via props. Events bubble up via callback props. Keep each component focused on one thing.

common_mistakes: Deep prop drilling — if data needs to pass through 5+ component levels, use Context API or state management instead of props

topics.name: Component Life Cycle

Lifecycle Phases: Class component lifecycle has 3 phases: Mounting (component created and added to DOM), Updating (re-render triggered by state/prop change), Unmounting (component removed from DOM).
Functional component equivalent: useEffect() hook covers all three phases depending on its dependency array.
Mounting Phase: constructor() → render() → (DOM updated) → componentDidMount(). componentDidMount(): runs ONCE after first render. Perfect for: API calls, event listener setup, third-party library init, setting timers.
Updating phase: Triggered by: setState(), new props from parent, forceUpdate(). Sequence: render() → (DOM updated) → componentDidUpdate(prevProps, prevState). Use prevProps/prevState to compare and avoid infinite loops.
Always check what changed: if (prevProps.userId !== this.props.userId) { // fetch new user data }
Unmounting Phase: componentWillUnmount(): runs just before component is removed. Cleanup: clear timers (clearInterval), remove event listeners, cancel API requests, disconnect WebSocket. Forgetting cleanup causes memory leaks.

required_diagrams: React lifecycle diagram: clean vertical flow diagram on white background. Three sections side by side. MOUNTING column: boxes top-to-bottom: constructor → render → DOM update → componentDidMount (marked 'API calls, timers, subscriptions'). UPDATING column: setState/new props → render → DOM update → componentDidUpdate (marked 'compare prevProps, avoid loops'). UNMOUNTING column: single box componentWillUnmount (marked 'cleanup: clear timers, remove listeners'). Arrows connecting boxes within each column. Plain black, white background.
common_mistakes: Not cleaning up in componentWillUnmount — timers, event listeners, and subscriptions continue running even after component is removed, causing memory leaks
Calling setState in componentDidUpdate without a condition — creates infinite render loop. Always check: if (prevProps.x !== this.props.x) before setState

topics.name: Event Handling in React
subtopics: Introduction to event handling in React
Handling user interactions (clicks, form submissions, input changes)

Introduction to event handling in React: React uses SyntheticEvent — a wrapper around the browser's native event. It normalises event behaviour across browsers. Works exactly like native events but cross-browser consistent.
Event names are camelCase in JSX: onClick (not onclick), onChange (not onchange), onSubmit (not onsubmit), onKeyDown, onMouseEnter, onFocus, onBlur.
Handling user interactions (clicks, form: onClick: fire function on click. event.preventDefault(): stop default browser behaviour (like form submit reloading page). event.stopPropagation(): stop event from bubbling up to parent elements.
onChange on input: fires on every keystroke. Use for controlled inputs where React state tracks input value in real time.
Binding event handlers to components: In functional components: no binding needed. Use arrow functions inline ({() => handler()}) or define handler in component body and reference ({handler}).
In class components: bind in constructor (this.handleClick = this.handleClick.bind(this)) OR use arrow function class property (handleClick = () => {}). Arrow class property is cleaner.

common_mistakes: onClick={handleClick()} — adding () calls the function immediately on render, not on click. Use onClick={handleClick} (no parentheses) to pass reference
Forgetting event.preventDefault() on form submit — page reloads and React state is lost

topics.name: Higher Order Components (HOC)
subtopics: Understanding the concept of Higher Order Components (HOCs)
Creating and implementing HOCs in React
Sharing common functionality and state with HOCs
Passing props and context to wrapped components

Understanding the concept of Higher Orde: HOC: a function that takes a component and returns a NEW component with added behaviour. Pattern: const EnhancedComponent = withSomething(OriginalComponent).
HOC is pure — it doesn't modify the original component. It wraps it. Use for cross-cutting concerns: auth check, loading state, analytics tracking, theme injection.
HOCs are a class-era pattern. In modern React, custom hooks are usually preferred for the same purpose — simpler, more composable.
Creating and implementing HOCs in React: Convention: HOC names start with 'with' — withAuth, withLoading, withLogger, withTheme.
HOC signature: function withSomething(WrappedComponent) { return function Enhanced(props) { /* add logic */ return <WrappedComponent {...props} extraProp={value} />; }; }
Composing components using HOCs: Multiple HOCs can be composed: const EnhancedPage = withAuth(withLoading(withLogger(PageComponent))). Applied right-to-left — Logger first, then Loading, then Auth.
compose() utility from Redux or lodash/fp flattens this: const EnhancedPage = compose(withAuth, withLoading, withLogger)(PageComponent)
Sharing common functionality and state w: HOC injects shared state or logic into wrapped component via props. Example: withFetch(url)(Component) — HOC manages fetching data and passes data, loading, error as props.
Passing props and context to wrapped com: Always spread props to wrapped component: <WrappedComponent {...props} />. Forgetting this breaks the wrapped component — it won't receive its own props.
Forward refs: HOC breaks ref forwarding. Use React.forwardRef inside HOC if the wrapped component needs to expose a ref.

common_mistakes: Forgetting {...props} spread — the wrapped component becomes isolated and receives no props from its parent
Defining HOC inside render method — creates a new component type on every render, causing unmount/mount cycles. Always define HOCs outside components

topics.name: Styling Approaches in React
subtopics: Using Popular UI Libraries (Tailwind CSS, Bootstrap)

Using Vanilla CSS: Import CSS file directly: import './App.css'. Classes applied via className prop. Global scope — .button in any CSS file affects ALL .button elements in app.
Problem: naming conflicts. Solution: use BEM naming (Block__Element--Modifier) or switch to CSS Modules.
Using Inline Styles: style prop takes a JavaScript OBJECT (not string). CSS property names are camelCase: backgroundColor not background-color. Values in strings: fontSize: '16px'.
Good for: dynamic styles that depend on JS variables, quick prototyping. Bad for: pseudo-classes (:hover), media queries, complex styles — use CSS Modules or styled-components instead.
Using CSS Modules: File named Component.module.css. Import: import styles from './Button.module.css'. Use: className={styles.button}. CSS classes are scoped to this component only — no global conflicts.
Best of both worlds: regular CSS syntax + automatic scope. Most recommended approach for plain React projects without a UI library.
Using Popular UI Libraries (Tailwind CSS: Tailwind CSS: utility-first. Apply pre-made classes directly in JSX: className='bg-blue-500 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded hover:bg-blue-600'. No CSS files needed. Install: npm install tailwindcss.

Bootstrap: pre-built component classes. className='btn btn-primary', className='container', className='row col-md-6'. Faster for standard UIs but less customisable. npm install bootstrap then import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css' in main.jsx.
Styled Components: CSS-in-JS library. Write actual CSS inside tagged template literals in JS. Each styled component is a React component with styles attached. Props create dynamic styles. Perfect for component libraries.
Install: npm install styled-components. Supports: theming via ThemeProvider, global styles (createGlobalStyle), animations (keyframes), media queries, pseudo-classes.

common_mistakes: Using inline styles for everything — they can't handle :hover, media queries, or keyframes. Use CSS Modules or styled-components for these
Importing regular CSS in large apps — global scope causes naming conflicts. Switch to CSS Modules or styled-components for component-level scoping

topics.name: Functional Component and Introduction to Hooks
subtopics: Syntax and structure of functional component

Introduction to functional component: Functional component: a plain JavaScript function that returns JSX. Since React 16.8 (2018) with hooks, functional components are the standard — they can manage state and side effects without being class components.
Syntax and structure of functional compo: Structure: import React (optional in React 17+), define function in PascalCase, return JSX. Use hooks at the top level of the function body — never inside loops, conditions, or nested functions (Rules of Hooks).
Introduction to react fragment: Fragment: <> </> or <React.Fragment> </React.Fragment>. Lets you return multiple elements without adding an extra DOM node. Useful when adding a wrapper div would break CSS (flex/grid layout).
What is state?: State: data that belongs to a component and can CHANGE over time. When state changes, React automatically re-renders the component with the new state value.
Think of state as the component's 'memory' — it remembers things between renders. Count of clicks, current tab selected, form input values, fetched API data — all are state.
Introduction to Hooks: Hooks: functions starting with 'use' that let functional components 'hook into' React features (state, lifecycle, context, etc.).
Rules of Hooks: (1) Only call hooks at the TOP LEVEL — not inside if/for/while. (2) Only call hooks from React functions — not regular JS functions. ESLint plugin enforces these rules.
useState(): useState(initialValue): returns [currentValue, setterFunction]. Re-renders component whenever setter is called with a new value.
Setter with function: setCount(prev => prev + 1) — use when new state depends on previous state. Avoids stale closure bugs in async code or batched updates.
useEffect(): useEffect(callback, [dependencies]): runs side effects after render. Side effects: API calls, timers, DOM manipulation, subscriptions.

Dependency array controls WHEN it runs: [] → only after first render (like componentDidMount). [id] → after first render AND whenever id changes. No array → after EVERY render (usually wrong). Return a cleanup function from useEffect to run on unmount (like componentWillUnmount).

common_mistakes: No dependency array in useEffect — runs after EVERY render, causing infinite loops if you setState inside it. Always provide dependency array
Missing cleanup in useEffect — memory leaks from timers, event listeners, subscriptions. Always return cleanup function

topics.name: Understanding Other Hooks

useRef(): useRef(initialValue): returns a mutable ref object {current: initialValue}. Two main uses: (1) Access DOM elements directly. (2) Store mutable value that persists across renders WITHOUT causing re-render.
Changing ref.current does NOT trigger re-render — unlike state. Use for: focus input, play/pause video, store previous value, store timer ID.
useReducer(): useReducer(reducer, initialState): alternative to useState for complex state logic. Returns [state, dispatch]. dispatch(action) triggers reducer(currentState, action) → returns new state.
Use when: multiple state values update together, next state depends on complex logic, actions have named types (like Redux). Keeps state logic centralised and testable.
useMemo(): useMemo(() => expensiveComputation(), [deps]): memoises the RESULT of a function. Only recomputes when dependencies change.
Use for: expensive computations (filtering/sorting large arrays), referential equality for objects passed as props. Don't overuse — memoisation itself has a cost.
useCallback(): useCallback(fn, [deps]): memoises the FUNCTION itself. Returns same function reference between renders if dependencies unchanged.
Use when: passing callback props to child components wrapped in React.memo — prevents unnecessary re-renders of children when parent re-renders.
Custom Hooks: Custom hook: a function starting with 'use' that calls other hooks. Extracts reusable stateful logic from components. NOT a component — returns data/functions, not JSX.
Pattern: useFetch, useLocalStorage, useDebounce, useForm, useWindowSize. Share logic between components without HOC or render props complexity.

common_mistakes: Using useMemo/useCallback everywhere — they add overhead. Only use when you have a measured performance problem or stable references for React.memo children
Forgetting dependencies in useMemo/useCallback — stale closures. ESLint exhaustive-deps rule catches these

topics.name: Implementing Global State (useContext)
subtopics: What is need of global state (Prop Drilling)?

What is need of global state (Prop Drill: Prop drilling: passing props through many intermediate components that don't use them, just to reach a deeply nested child. Example: User data from App → Layout → Sidebar → ProfileWidget — Layout and Sidebar don't use it, just pass it down.
Solution: React Context — store data at a high level, any component in the tree can access it directly without passing through every level.
useContext() hook: Three steps: (1) createContext() — create the context object. (2) Context.Provider value={...} — wrap component tree, provide the value. (3) useContext(Context) — consume value in any descendant component.
Context re-renders ALL consumers when value changes — be careful with frequently changing data. Split contexts by concern: AuthContext, ThemeContext, CartContext separately.

common_mistakes: Putting everything in one context — causes all consumers to re-render on any change. Split into AuthContext, CartContext, ThemeContext etc.
Using Context for all state — Context is for truly global state (auth, theme, language). Local state stays in components, server state in React Query

topics.name: Conditional Rendering and Lists
subtopics: Conditional Rendering using ternary operator, Logical and operator, switch statement, enum

Conditional Rendering using ternary oper: Four patterns: (1) Ternary: {isLoading ? <Spinner /> : <Content />}. (2) Logical &&: {error && <ErrorMsg />} — renders right side only if left is truthy. (3) Early return: if (!data) return <Loader />. (4) Enum/object map: STATUS_COMPONENTS[status] for multiple states.
Enum pattern avoids nested ternaries. Create an object mapping each status to its component, look up by key.
Rendering list through loops: Use .map() to render arrays. Every rendered element in a list MUST have a unique key prop. Key must be stable (from database ID), unique among siblings, and consistent between renders.
Rendering empty state: {items.length === 0 ? <EmptyState /> : items.map(...)}. Rendering loading skeleton: Array.from({length: 5}).map((_, i) => <Skeleton key={i} />).

common_mistakes: Using 0 && <Component /> — renders the number 0 on screen. Use 0 > 0 && or Boolean(0) && to be safe
Using Math.random() as key — generates new random key every render, forcing React to unmount/remount every list item

topics.name: Converting Class to Functional Component
subtopics: How to convert class based component to functional component?
Functional component vs Class based component

How to convert class based component to: Mapping: this.state / setState → useState(). componentDidMount → useEffect(() => {}, []). componentDidUpdate → useEffect(() => {}, [deps]). componentWillUnmount → return () => {} inside useEffect. this.props → function parameter { ...props }.
Binding event handlers → not needed in functional components. Arrow function class properties → regular functions or arrow functions in component body.
Functional component vs Class based comp: Functional + hooks is the clear winner for new code: less boilerplate, no this confusion, logic reuse via custom hooks, better tree shaking, easier testing.
Class components aren't going away — React team has no plans to remove them. But no new features are being added only to class components.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to handle cleanup when converting componentWillUnmount — the return function inside useEffect is the equivalent and is often missed

topics.name: React Router v7

Why we need routing: SPA has one HTML page. Without routing, URL never changes — no bookmarking, no back button, no sharing links to specific pages. React Router makes URL changes without page reload.
React Router v7 (also called Remix v3 — they merged) is the modern standard. Install: npm install react-router-dom.
<a> vs <Link>: <a href='/about'>: full page reload — browser makes new request to server. Loses React state. Bad for SPA.
<Link to='/about'>: React Router's component. Updates URL and swaps component WITHOUT page reload. State preserved. Always use Link for internal navigation. <a> only for external URLs.
createBrowserRouter & RouterProvider: v7 data router API: createBrowserRouter() defines routes as config objects. RouterProvider renders the router. Replaces old <BrowserRouter><Routes><Route> pattern.
Data router enables: loaders (fetch data before rendering), actions (handle form submissions), error boundaries per route — all built in.
Basic and nested routes: Nested routes: child routes render inside parent's <Outlet />. Layout routes: parent provides shared UI (Navbar, Sidebar) via Outlet. Child routes only need to define their unique content.
Index route: default child when parent path matches exactly. errorElement: component shown when route throws error.

common_mistakes: Using <a> for internal navigation — causes full page reload and loses all React state. Always use <Link> or <NavLink>
Forgetting <Outlet /> in layout component — child routes render nowhere, page appears blank

topics.name: Navigation, Route Data and Advanced Routing

Route params (useParams): Dynamic segments: path: ':productId' in route config. useParams() hook: const { productId } = useParams() — extracts the actual value from current URL.
Multiple params: path: ':category/:productId' → const { category, productId } = useParams().
Query strings: Query strings: ?sort=price&order=asc. useSearchParams() hook returns [searchParams, setSearchParams]. Read: searchParams.get('sort'). Write: setSearchParams({ sort: 'name' }).
Prefer query strings over state for: filters, sort order, search query, page number — makes URL shareable and bookmarkable.
useRouteError handling: errorElement at route level shows custom error UI when that route's loader/action/component throws. useRouteError() hook gets the error object inside errorElement.
Layout routes: Layout route: path-less route with element={<Layout />} and children. Layout renders persistent UI (Navbar, Sidebar) and <Outlet /> for child content.
Multiple layouts: public layout (minimal, for login/signup) and app layout (full nav, for authenticated pages).
Protected routes (auth): Wrap private routes with an auth-check component. If not authenticated, redirect to login using <Navigate to='/login' replace />. If authenticated, render <Outlet /> for child routes.
The replace prop replaces current history entry so back button goes to page before login, not back to the failed protected route.
Redirection & fallback UI: Programmatic navigation: const navigate = useNavigate(). navigate('/dashboard') to redirect. navigate(-1) to go back. navigate('/login', { replace: true }) to replace history.
Fallback: path: '*' catches all unmatched routes → show 404 page.

common_mistakes: Not using replace on post-login redirect — user can click back to get to login page while logged in. Always use navigate('/dashboard', { replace: true })
Putting auth logic in every protected page — use a ProtectedRoute wrapper component that handles auth for all child routes at once

topics.name: Form Handling in React
subtopics: Managing Forms With React Hook Form

Introduction: React handles forms differently from HTML. HTML forms maintain their own state in the DOM. React recommends controlled components — React state is the single source of truth for input values.
Building a Form: Form elements: <input>, <textarea>, <select>, <checkbox>, <radio>. Each needs value (or checked for checkbox) and onChange handler for controlled pattern.
Handling Form Submission: onSubmit on <form> element. Always call e.preventDefault() to stop browser's default form submission (page reload). Collect values from state and send to API.
Accessing Input Fields: Controlled: values in React state — access via state variable. Uncontrolled: values in DOM — access via useRef(). ref.current.value gives current input value.
Controlled Components: value={state} + onChange={setter} on every input. React state is the single truth. Good for: real-time validation, disabling submit until valid, formatting input as user types.
Uncontrolled components: No value/onChange — input manages its own value in DOM. Access via useRef(). Good for: simple forms where you only need values on submit, file inputs (always uncontrolled), integrating with non-React libraries.
Managing Forms With React Hook Form: React Hook Form (RHF): performance-focused form library. Minimal re-renders (only changed fields re-render, not whole form). register() connects inputs. handleSubmit() wraps submit handler. formState.errors for validation errors.
Install: npm install react-hook-form. Works with native inputs and UI libraries (MUI, Chakra, etc.).
Applying Validation: RHF validation options in register(): required, minLength, maxLength, pattern (regex), min, max, validate (custom function). Schema validation: use Zod or Yup with @hookform/resolvers for complex rules.

common_mistakes: Forgetting e.preventDefault() on form submit — page reloads, React state lost
Controlled input without onChange handler — React warns 'You provided a value prop without an onChange handler'. Either add onChange or use defaultValue for uncontrolled

topics.name: Making API Calls in React

Axios: Axios: HTTP client library. Benefits over fetch: automatic JSON parsing, request/response interceptors, better error handling (throws on 4xx/5xx), request cancellation, timeout support.
Install: npm install axios. Create an instance with base URL and headers for reuse across the app.
React Query (TanStack Query): React Query (now TanStack Query): server state management library. Handles caching, background refetching, loading/error states, pagination, infinite scroll — automatically.
Install: npm install @tanstack/react-query. Wrap app with QueryClientProvider. Use useQuery for fetching, useMutation for POST/PUT/DELETE.
SWR (stale-while-revalidate): SWR by Vercel: lightweight data fetching. Shows stale (cached) data immediately while revalidating (fetching fresh data) in background. useSWR(key, fetcher) — simple API.
Install: npm install swr. Best for: simple data fetching where you want instant cached response + background sync.
RTK Query: RTK Query: built into Redux Toolkit. If you're already using Redux, use RTK Query instead of React Query. Generates hooks from API endpoints definition.
Install: included in @reduxjs/toolkit. Define API with createApi(), generates useGetUsersQuery, useCreateUserMutation etc. automatically.
React toastify (notifications): React Toastify: toast notification library. Show success/error/info/warning toasts for API response feedback. Install: npm install react-toastify.
Add <ToastContainer /> once at root. Call toast.success(), toast.error(), toast.info() from anywhere in the app.

common_mistakes: Making API calls directly inside components without React Query/SWR — you manually handle loading, error, caching, refetching. Use a data fetching library instead
Not cancelling API requests when component unmounts — causes 'setState on unmounted component' warning. React Query handles this automatically

topics.name: Flux Architecture

Introduction to flux: Flux: application architecture pattern created by Facebook alongside React. Solves state management in large apps where multiple components need same shared data. Not a library — a pattern.
Flux architecture overview: Unidirectional data flow: Action → Dispatcher → Store → View → Action. Data flows in ONE direction only — predictable, debuggable.
Redux is the most popular implementation of Flux pattern. Redux Toolkit is the modern way to write Redux.
Actions: Action: a plain JavaScript object describing what happened. Must have a type property. Example: { type: 'ADD_ITEM', payload: { id: 1, name: 'Laptop' } }.
Action creators: Action creator: a function that creates and returns an action object. Keeps action creation consistent and testable.
Example: const addItem = (item) => ({ type: 'ADD_ITEM', payload: item }).
Dispatcher: Dispatcher: central hub that receives actions and dispatches them to all registered stores. In Redux: the store's dispatch function serves this role.
Stores: Store: holds application state. Only way to change state is to dispatch an action. State is read-only — reducer creates new state objects.
Async actions: Async operations (API calls) can't be handled in plain actions (synchronous). Solutions: middleware. Redux Thunk (dispatch functions), Redux Saga (generator functions), RTK createAsyncThunk (built into Redux Toolkit).

required_diagrams: Flux architecture diagram: clean diagram on white background. Four rectangular boxes in a circular flow. 1) User View (component). 2) Action Creator (creates action object). 3) Dispatcher (central hub). 4) Store (holds state). Arrows connecting in order: View → Action Creator → Dispatcher → Store → View. Label each arrow with what flows: 'User interaction', 'action object', 'dispatch', 'state update triggers re-render'. Plain black, white background.
common_mistakes: Choosing Redux for small apps — Context API + useReducer is often enough. Add Redux only when app genuinely needs it

topics.name: Redux and Redux Toolkit

What is Redux?: Redux: predictable state container for JavaScript apps. Single store holds entire app state. State is read-only. Changes only via pure reducer functions. Excellent DevTools for time-travel debugging.
Why one need Redux?: Need Redux when: multiple unrelated components share state, state updates are complex (many actions), you need time-travel debugging, team needs predictable state management at scale.
Don't need Redux for: small apps, state that's local to one component subtree, server state (use React Query instead).
Redux actions and action creators: Redux Toolkit's createSlice automatically generates action creators. No need to write them manually.
Reducer functions: Reducer: pure function (currentState, action) => newState. Must be pure — no side effects, no mutation, same input always gives same output.
Redux Toolkit uses Immer under the hood — allows 'mutating' syntax in reducers while actually creating new state objects. state.count++ is safe inside RTK reducers.
Context api vs redux: Context API: built-in, simple, good for low-frequency updates (auth, theme). Redux: external library, more boilerplate, but DevTools, middleware, complex update patterns, better performance for frequent updates.
Need of redux toolkit: RTK was created because vanilla Redux had too much boilerplate: create action types constants, action creators, reducers separately, configure store with compose(). RTK reduces this to one createSlice call.
Redux vs Redux Toolkit: Vanilla Redux: many files, manual action types, switch-case reducers, complex store setup. RTK: createSlice (combines action types + creators + reducer), configureStore (includes Redux DevTools, Thunk middleware automatically).
Redux Toolkit thunk: createAsyncThunk: handles async operations. Automatically dispatches pending/fulfilled/rejected action types. Use extraReducers in slice to handle these states.
Redux hooks: useSelector(selector): reads state from Redux store. Re-renders only when selected state changes. useDispatch(): returns dispatch function to send actions to store.
Redux thunk: Redux Thunk middleware: allows action creators to return FUNCTIONS instead of plain objects. Function receives dispatch — can dispatch multiple actions asynchronously.
Introduction to redux saga: Redux Saga: middleware using generator functions (function*) to handle side effects. More powerful than Thunk for complex async flows: race conditions, concurrent requests, retry logic, WebSocket handling.
Install: npm install redux-saga. Complex setup but very powerful for enterprise-level async orchestration.

common_mistakes: Mutating state in vanilla Redux reducers (without RTK) — return {...state, count: state.count+1} NOT state.count++. RTK's Immer makes mutation syntax safe only inside createSlice
Over-fetching from Redux — not everything belongs in Redux store. Form state, UI state (modal open/close), server data (use React Query) should stay local

topics.name: React Performance and Advanced Patterns
subtopics: Introduction to code optimization (Minification, Tree shaking, Code splitting)
Lifecycle methods to handle error boundaries

Suspense: Suspense: React component that shows fallback UI while waiting for something to load. Originally for lazy-loaded components, now extended to async data fetching (React 18).
<Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}><LazyComponent /></Suspense> — shows Spinner until LazyComponent's code is downloaded.
Lazy loading: React.lazy(() => import('./HeavyComponent')): dynamically import component — its JS code is NOT included in main bundle. Only downloaded when component is first needed.
Reduces initial bundle size → faster first load. Combine with Suspense for fallback UI while component code downloads.
React Portals: ReactDOM.createPortal(children, domNode): renders children into a different DOM node than parent. Children appear in DOM outside React root (e.g., document.body) but still behave as React children (events bubble correctly).
Use for: modals, tooltips, dropdowns — components that need to visually 'escape' CSS overflow:hidden or z-index of parent containers.
Infinite scroll: IntersectionObserver API: watches when a 'sentinel' element enters viewport → triggers loading next page. More performant than scroll event listeners.
React Query provides useInfiniteQuery hook for infinite scroll with automatic page management.
React fibre: React Fiber: complete rewrite of React's reconciliation algorithm (React 16+). Enables: incremental rendering (split work into chunks), prioritisation (user interactions > data updates), concurrency (React 18 concurrent mode).
Fiber makes React's 'concurrent features' possible: Suspense, transitions (startTransition), useDeferredValue — UI stays responsive during heavy computation.
Introduction to code optimization (Minif: Minification: remove whitespace, shorten variable names in production build. Vite/webpack do this automatically with npm run build.
Tree shaking: dead code elimination. Bundler removes unused exports. Use named imports (import { useQuery } from) not default whole libraries.
Code splitting: split bundle into multiple chunks. Route-level splitting (most impactful) via React.lazy. Component-level for heavy components (charts, editors).
Introduction to component optimization (: Virtualization: render only visible items in long lists. 10,000 items in DOM = slow. Virtualisation renders only ~20 visible items — huge performance gain. Libraries: react-virtual, react-window.
Memoization: React.memo wraps component — prevents re-render if props unchanged. useMemo memoises computed values. useCallback memoises functions. Profile FIRST, memoize only where needed.
Image optimization: Lazy load images: loading='lazy' attribute on — browser only downloads when near viewport. Use appropriate sizes — don't serve 4K images for 200px thumbnails.
Modern formats: WebP (30% smaller than JPEG). Use srcSet for responsive images. In Next.js: <Image /> component handles all of this automatically.
Introduction to error boundaries: Error boundaries: class components that catch JavaScript errors in their child component tree and display fallback UI instead of crashing the whole app.
Two lifecycle methods: static getDerivedStateFromError() — updates state to show fallback UI. componentDidCatch() — logs error to error tracking service (Sentry).
Lifecycle methods to handle error bounda: static getDerivedStateFromError(error): static method, returns state update to trigger fallback render. Called during rendering phase.
componentDidCatch(error, info): side effects allowed here (logging). info.componentStack shows which component threw the error. Use react-error-boundary library for a hook-friendly wrapper.

common_mistakes: Error boundaries don't catch: event handler errors, async errors, errors in SSR. For events, use try/catch inside the handler
Memoizing everything — React.memo has overhead. Only use when you've measured re-render performance issues

topics.name: Debugging React with Chrome DevTools
subtopics: Inspecting the react components in element panel
Viewing and modifying the components props and state
Analysing and modifying the component styles
Debugging react component rendering and performance
Utilizing the react dev tools extension
Debugging react events and event handlers
Inspecting react context and redux state
Working with react hooks in dev tool
Profiling react component and optimizing the performance
Analyzing the react component updates and re-render
Debugging react router and routing issue

Introduction to chrome dev tools: Open DevTools: F12 or right-click → Inspect. Key panels: Elements (DOM), Console (JS output/errors), Network (API requests), Sources (code + breakpoints), Performance (profiling), Application (localStorage, cookies).
Inspecting the react components in eleme: Elements panel shows real DOM — not React component tree. Install React DevTools Chrome extension for component-level inspection.
React DevTools adds Components and Profiler panels. Components panel shows React component tree with props and state.
Viewing and modifying the components pro: React DevTools Components panel: click any component → see its props and state on the right. Can EDIT values directly — changes reflected immediately in UI. Great for testing edge cases without changing code.
Debugging react component hierarchy: Components panel shows parent-child relationships. Use search to find a specific component by name. 'Inspect component' button: click element in page → jumps to its component in React DevTools.
Analysing and modifying the component st: Elements panel → Styles pane: see all CSS applied to selected DOM element. Toggle properties on/off. Add new CSS rules. Computed tab: see final computed values. Changes are temporary (lost on refresh).
Debugging react component rendering and: React DevTools Profiler: Record → interact with app → Stop. See which components rendered, how long each took. Flame graph shows rendering hierarchy. Ranked chart shows slowest components.
Utilizing the react dev tools extension: Install: Chrome Web Store → 'React Developer Tools'. Features: component tree, props/state inspection, hook values, context values, profiler, highlight updates (flashes when component re-renders).
Debugging react events and event handler: Console: console.log inside event handlers. Network panel: see API requests triggered by events. Breakpoints: Sources panel → click line number → code pauses at that line when executed.
Inspecting react context and redux state: React DevTools: Components panel → find Context.Provider → see context value. Redux DevTools extension: shows action history, state tree, time-travel debugging (jump back to any previous state).
Working with react hooks in dev tool: React DevTools Components panel: click functional component → see all hooks listed with current values. useState values, useRef current, custom hook return values — all visible and editable.
Profiling react component and optimizing: Profiler steps: Open React DevTools → Profiler tab → click Record (circle) → interact with app → click Stop → analyze. Look for: unexpected re-renders, slow renders (>16ms causes frame drop), waterfall patterns.
Analyzing the react component updates an: Enable 'Highlight updates' in React DevTools settings → blue flashes on every render. Too many flashes = too many re-renders. Add React.memo, useCallback, useMemo to reduce re-renders.
Debugging react router and routing issue: Network tab: check if API requests are being made. Console: React Router logs warnings for misconfigurations. Check: route path matches URL exactly, nested routes have <Outlet />, protected routes redirect correctly.

common_mistakes: Relying only on console.log for debugging — use breakpoints in Sources panel and React DevTools for component-level debugging
Not installing React DevTools extension — the default Chrome DevTools shows DOM, not React components. React DevTools is essential

topics.name: Testing React Applications

Introduction to testing: Testing: automated verification that code works as expected. Catches regressions (previously working features breaking after changes). Increases confidence to refactor and add features.
Types of testing (Unit, Integration, End: Unit: test one function/component in isolation. Fast, many. Integration: test multiple components/modules working together. E2E (End-to-End): test full user flows in real browser (Cypress, Playwright). Slow, few.
Introduction to testing pyramid: Testing pyramid: many unit tests (fast, cheap) at base, fewer integration tests in middle, very few E2E tests at top (slow, expensive). Aim for most coverage from unit and integration tests.
Introduction to Jest: Jest: JavaScript testing framework. Built into Create React App. Features: test runner, assertion library (expect), mocking, coverage reports. Vite: add Vitest (Jest-compatible, faster).
File naming: MyComponent.test.jsx or MyComponent.spec.jsx. Jest auto-discovers test files. Run: npm test.
Introduction to react testing library: React Testing Library (RTL): renders components in real DOM (jsdom), queries by user-visible attributes. Philosophy: test how users USE the app, not implementation details.
Queries (prefer in order): getByRole (most accessible), getByLabelText, getByPlaceholderText, getByText, getByDisplayValue, getByTestId (last resort).
Testing component rendering: render(<Component />) from RTL. screen.getByText(), screen.getByRole(), screen.queryByText() (returns null if not found, doesn't throw). screen.debug() prints current DOM.
Simulating UI: userEvent (preferred over fireEvent): simulates real user interactions. userEvent.click(), userEvent.type(), userEvent.selectOptions(). More realistic than fireEvent — triggers all intermediate events.
Snapshot testing: Snapshot test: renders component, saves serialised output to .snap file. Future runs compare against snapshot — any change fails test. Use sparingly — can become maintenance burden. Good for: intentionally stable UI (design system components).
Mocking: jest.fn(): create mock function. jest.spyOn(object, 'method'): spy on existing method. jest.mock('./module'): replace entire module with mock. Mock external APIs in tests — never make real HTTP requests.
Testing actions: RTK action creators are plain functions — test that they return correct action object. expect(counterSlice.actions.increment()).toEqual({ type: 'counter/increment' }).
Testing reducers: Reducers are pure functions — easy to test. Call reducer with state and action, assert returned state. No need to render anything.
Testing thunks: Use redux-mock-store or test with real store. For createAsyncThunk: mock fetch, dispatch thunk, assert dispatched actions.
Testing component interaction: Integration test: render parent with children, interact with UI, assert both components responded correctly. Tests real component composition.
Mocking API calls: msw (Mock Service Worker): intercept network requests at network level. Most realistic — your code makes real fetch calls, msw intercepts and returns mock data. Perfect for integration tests.
Testing custom hooks: renderHook from RTL: const { result } = renderHook(() => useCounter()). Access return value via result.current. Wrap state updates in act().
Mocking react hooks: jest.mock the module exporting the hook. Or use mockReturnValue to control hook return. Better: test custom hooks directly instead of mocking them.
Setting up continuous integration: CI runs tests automatically on every git push. GitHub Actions: create .github/workflows/test.yml. Runs npm test -- --ci --coverage on every PR. Blocks merge if tests fail.
Test coverage: npm test -- --coverage: generates HTML coverage report. Shows which lines/branches are tested. Aim for 70-80% coverage on business logic. Don't chase 100% — UI snapshot tests add little value.

common_mistakes: Testing implementation details (internal state variable names, method calls) — tests break on refactoring. Test observable behavior: what the user sees and can interact with
Not wrapping async state updates in waitFor() — test passes before async operation completes. Always await async interactions

topics.name: Building and Deploying React App
subtopics: How to build a react app

How to build a react app: npm run build (or yarn build): creates optimised production build in /dist (Vite) or /build (CRA). Minifies JS, removes dev code, splits bundles.
What changes in production: no React StrictMode double-renders, no PropTypes warnings, no source maps by default, smaller bundle size. Always test production build before deploying.
Using Vercel, Netlify & Github Pages: Vercel (easiest for React): connect GitHub repo → auto-deploys on every push. Zero config for Vite/CRA. Custom domain free. Environment variables in dashboard. Best choice for React apps.
Netlify: similar to Vercel. Great for static sites. Drag-and-drop deploy: drag /dist folder to netlify.com. Or connect GitHub for auto-deploy.
GitHub Pages: free hosting from GitHub repo. For SPAs: need to handle client-side routing (copy index.html to 404.html trick or use HashRouter). npm install gh-pages. Add scripts in package.json.

topics.required_code:
# ===== Production Build =====
npm run build
# Creates /dist folder (Vite) or /build (CRA) with:
# - index.html
# - assets/index-[hash].js  (minified, hashed)
# - assets/index-[hash].css

# Preview production build locally before deploying:
npm run preview  # Vite
npx serve build  # CRA

# ===== Deploy to Vercel =====
# Option 1: Vercel CLI
npm install -g vercel
vercel login
vercel          # deploy from project root
vercel --prod   # deploy to production URL

# Option 2: Connect GitHub (recommended)
# 1. Push code to GitHub
# 2. Go to vercel.com → New Project → Import GitHub repo
# 3. Vercel auto-detects Vite/CRA, sets build command
# 4. Every push to main auto-deploys

# ===== Deploy to Netlify =====
# Option 1: CLI
npm install -g netlify-cli
netlify login
npm run build
netlify deploy --prod --dir=dist  # Vite

# Option 2: Drag & drop
# npm run build → drag /dist folder to netlify.com

# ===== Deploy to GitHub Pages =====
# Install gh-pages
npm install --save-dev gh-pages

# Add to package.json:
# "homepage": "https://username.github.io/repo-name",
# "scripts": {
#   "predeploy": "npm run build",
#   "deploy": "gh-pages -d dist"
# }

npm run deploy  # builds and pushes to gh-pages branch

# ===== Environment Variables =====
# .env file (never commit to git!)
# Vite: prefix with VITE_
VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com
VITE_APP_TITLE=My App

# Access in code:
# const apiUrl = import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL;  // Vite
# const apiUrl = process.env.REACT_APP_API_URL; // CRA (prefix REACT_APP_)

# On Vercel/Netlify: add env vars in dashboard Settings → Environment Variables
common_mistakes: Committing .env file with secrets to GitHub — add .env to .gitignore. Use .env.example for documentation
Using HashRouter for GitHub Pages without understanding the tradeoff — URLs look like /#/about instead of /about. Vercel/Netlify support BrowserRouter natively
